metadata.title: Build a Diff-First Change Auditor with Claude

beats.title: Liam cold open — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: Olá, Liam — in for Bear. Agentic CLI — build it, measure it, take it apart? Can you explain it, Bear?

props.command: Agentic CLI — build it, measure it, take it apart?
props.segment: Build a Diff-First Change Auditor with

beats.narration_text: Nik Bear Brown. Agentic CLI — build it, measure it, take it apart.

The most expensive agent-assisted mistake is applying a broad change that touched forty-seven files when you only meant to touch three — and the diff-first pattern stops it at the intent declaration stage. Before any edit: state what you intend to change and why. The declaration is the review gate.

shot.notes: terminal showing 'files modified: 47' counter in crimson vs 'files modified: 3' in green; caption: 'the most expensive mistake = blast radius surprise'; 'diff-first = intent before edits'

beats.narration_text: Ask for the intent declaration. In the terminal: claude — before making any edits: state exactly what you intend to change and why. List each file you plan to modify, the specific change, and the reason. Do not make any edits yet.

props.command:
claude "Before making any edits: state exactly
  what you intend to change and why.
  List: (1) each file to modify
  (2) specific change in each file
  (3) reason the change is needed.
  No edits yet." < task_description.md

beats.narration_text: Read the auditor. It sends the intent-declaration prompt to Claude, displays the result file by file, then shows a review prompt: approve, reject specific files, or abort all. Only after explicit approval does the edit loop run. The script tracks files modified before and after the gate to compute blast radius.

props.code:
# diff_first_auditor.py — Claude Code output
import subprocess, sys

DECL_PROMPT = (
    "Before any edits: list each file to modify, "
    "the specific change, and the reason. "
    "No edits yet.")

task = open(sys.argv[1]).read()
declaration = subprocess.run(
    ['claude', DECL_PROMPT + '\n' + task],
    capture_output=True, text=True).stdout

print(declaration)
print('\n--- REVIEW GATE ---')
print('[A]pprove  [R]eject file  [X]abort')
choice = input('> ').strip().upper()

if choice == 'A':
    subprocess.run(['claude', 'Now apply the edits.\n' + task])
elif choice == 'X':
    print('Aborted. Zero files modified.')

beats.narration_text: The declaration appears file by file. config dot py: update line forty-seven to use the new API endpoint URL. tests slash test underscore config dot py: add endpoint validation test. requirements dot txt: pin requests to two-point-thirty-one. Three files. Clear. Then — line eleven: utils slash auth dot py: refactor token cache to support multi-tenant. That was not in the task.

shot.notes: terminal output: declaration scrolling file by file — config.py (green), test_config.py (green), requirements.txt (green), then utils/auth.py (crimson, unexpected); 'REVIEW GATE' prompt at bottom with A/R/X options

beats.narration_text: Reject the unexpected file. In the terminal: R — then specify utils slash auth dot py. The auditor re-declares without it and shows the narrowed change set. Then approve.

props.command:
> R
Reject which file? utils/auth.py

claude "Re-declare without utils/auth.py.
  Apply only the approved 3-file change."

beats.narration_text: The narrowed edit runs. Files modified: three. The auth refactor never happens. Without the gate, it would have happened silently, alongside the config change, and the test suite would have caught it two commits later — or not at all.

shot.notes: two counters side by side: WITHOUT GATE 'files modified: 4' (crimson, includes auth.py); WITH GATE 'files modified: 3' (green, exact scope); caption: 'caught at zero-edit cost'

beats.narration_text: The diff-first pattern converts a broad instruction into a specific contract before execution. The moment of review is when the cost of catching a misinterpretation is lowest — zero edits made — and the value of catching it is highest — potentially dozens of files avoided. The declaration is not overhead. It is the product.

shot.notes: teardown card: 'diff-first = instruction → contract before execution'; timeline: DECLARE (cost: 0 edits) → REVIEW → EDIT; two bullets: 'lowest cost to catch: at declaration' / 'highest value to catch: before blast radius'

beats.narration_text: Your move: wrap your next agent coding task with the intent-declaration gate. Before approving, count the files in the list. If the number surprises you, you just saved yourself a revert. That is the series — nine patterns, one discipline.

shot.notes: next steps: '1) use intent-declaration gate for all agent edits  2) count files before approving  3) reject and re-declare if scope surprises you'; closing card: '9 patterns / 1 discipline: state intent before action'

beats.title: Liam verdict — ClaudeVerdictArtifact
beats.narration_text: Here's what the body just demonstrated. The diff-first pattern converts a broad instruction into a specific contract before execution. The moment of review is when the cost of catching a misinterpretation is lowest — zero edits made — and the value of catching it is highest — potentially dozens of files avoided. The declaration is not overhead. It is the product. Your move: wrap your next agent coding task with the intent-declaration gate. Before approving, count the files in the list. If the number surprises you, you just saved yourself a revert. That is the series — nine patterns, one discipline.

beats.narration_text: Take this prompt, run it on your own — pick any cancer type or clinical scenario you know about and ask how this mechanism applies there.
